article_title: JWST Maps a Dawn-Dusk Divide on Ultra-Hot Jupiter WASP-121 b, Where the Evening Sky Runs Hotter and Water Falls Apart
summary: Submission approvable with 2 minor warning(s) — file a corrections record on merge

content_preview.title: JWST Maps a Dawn-Dusk Divide on Ultra-Hot Jupiter WASP-121 b, Where the Evening Sky Runs Hotter and Water Falls Apart
content_preview.summary: A rotational-transit study in Nature Astronomy finds WASP-121 b's evening terminator is hotter and more expanded than its morning side, with upper-atmosphere water broken into its constituent atoms.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Astronomers using the James Webb Space Telescope have measured a sharp difference between the morning and evening edges of an ultra-hot exoplanet, finding that the world's evening sky is hotter and more puffed-up than its dawn. The planet, WASP-121 b, is a gas giant whose dayside reaches around 2,770 Kelvin while its nightside sits closer to about 1,000 Kelvin, [according to Phys.org](https://phys.org/news/2026-06-jwst-reveals-dawn-dusk-atmosphere.html). The study, led by Cyril Gapp...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt
1 source(s) blocked bot access — sources likely valid but not archivable automatically

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News piece (719 words, within the 400-1200 range). Clear Overview/What We Know/What We Don't Know/Analysis arc, neutral tone, appropriate technical depth. The 'What We Don't Know' section honestly flags the silicate clouds as a model-dependent inference and notes that the wind-speed and exact terminator temperature-gap magnitudes are not quantified in the public coverage — good editorial restraint.
editor_notes.source_verification: All four source snapshots inspected on disk. (1) phys.org [source-0]: snapshot FAILED with HTTP 403 (bot-blocked); file is null — unverifiable directly. (2) eurekalert.org [source-1.html.gz]: snapshot returned an AWS WAF bot-challenge / 'JavaScript is disabled' interstitial (202, 2179 bytes), NOT the release content — effectively bot-blocked and unverifiable. (3) sciencedaily.com [source-2.html.gz]: full readable article (200), confirms nearly every claim. (4) thebrighterside.news [source-3.html.gz]: full readable article (200), corroborates the central findings. CRITICAL CHECK: every specific the article attributes to the two blocked sources is independently corroborated in the two readable snapshots — 2,770 K dayside / ~1,000 K nightside (Brighter Side + ScienceDaily), 1.9 stellar diameters (Brighter Side), ~30 degrees rotation during transit (Brighter Side), 30-hour synchronized rotation (Brighter Side), NIRSpec instrument (Brighter Side + ScienceDaily), Nature Astronomy journal (Brighter Side + ScienceDaily), evening terminator hotter/more expanded (ScienceDaily + Brighter Side), water thermally dissociating into constituent parts (ScienceDaily + Brighter Side), silicate/mineral clouds as model improvement (ScienceDaily + Brighter Side), CO signal as a temperature effect (ScienceDaily), team members Cyril Gapp/Thomas M. Evans-Soma (Newcastle, Australia)/Eva-Maria Ahrer of MPIA (ScienceDaily). The headline, summary and Overview lead rest entirely on these readable, corroborated sources — NOT on the paywalled/blocked ones (Rule 9 satisfied). The only specific resting solely on a blocked source is the exact Nature Astronomy publication date 'June 10, 2026' (attributed to EurekAlert): it does NOT appear in either readable snapshot (ScienceDaily is dated June 11, 2026; Brighter Side published June 12, 2026) — see corrections record.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: WASP-121 b designation verified verbatim across all readable sources (with the space before 'b'). Direct quote — 'With its unprecedented observational quality, JWST gives us the most detailed glimpses into distant planets to date... By measuring how star light absorption changes as WASP-121 b rotates, we probe its atmosphere longitude by longitude' — appears VERBATIM in both ScienceDaily and Brighter Side and is correctly attributed to Cyril Gapp (MPIA). Dawn-dusk direction is correct: EVENING terminator hotter/more expanded, driven by eastward winds — matches sources exactly. Dropped-fact check: the 'iron vaporizes' framing is genuinely ABSENT from the body (no occurrence of 'iron'); no specific light-year distance figure is asserted (the Analysis only says 'hundreds of light-years away' generically, which needs no source). No fabricated specifics detected.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No sensationalism, no AI self-reference, no editorializing. Attributive phrasing ('according to', 'reports', 'notes') is used consistently.
editor_notes.originality: New topic. The only adjacent recent astronomy piece in 2026-06 is the AB Aurigae protoplanetary-disk rotation article; this is a distinct study (JWST transmission spectroscopy of WASP-121 b), no overlap of subject, sources, or angle.
concerns: Two of four source snapshots are bot-blocked: phys.org returned HTTP 403 (null snapshot) and EurekAlert returned an AWS WAF challenge page rather than the release content. Both are reputable allowlisted domains and every fact they are cited for is corroborated by the two readable sources, so this does not undermine the article — but neither was directly verifiable from its snapshot.

The exact Nature Astronomy publication date 'June 10, 2026' rests solely on the bot-blocked EurekAlert snapshot and is not corroborated in either readable source (ScienceDaily dated June 11, 2026; Brighter Side published June 12, 2026). Filed as a clarification in the corrections record.

thebrighterside.news is not on config/source_allowlist.txt. It returned full, on-topic content republishing the MPIA release and is used only for non-extraordinary, independently-corroborated claims, so it is acceptable here; allowlist not modified per review scope.
recommendations: If the precise Nature Astronomy publication date matters for the record, confirm it against the journal DOI page once the EurekAlert WAF challenge can be cleared.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS. The article is substantively sound, neutral, well-sourced and original; its headline, summary and lead rest entirely on readable, independently-corroborated sources. The single recoverable issue — the precise 'June 10, 2026' Nature Astronomy publication date, which rests only on a bot-blocked EurekAlert snapshot and conflicts with the readable sources' own June 11/June 12 dates — is honestly captured in the corrections record and does not affect any central claim.
